---
title: "LRPITT11: ITT analysis of nonword reading (N) — floor-rule branch"

date: last-modified
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss Z"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

**In plain terms.** Did the children offered the intervention read more nonwords — decoding unfamiliar letter strings (a heavily floored skill, so the headline is whether a child comes off the floor at all) than those who were not — and how large, and how certain, is that difference? Because group assignment was randomised, this is a genuine causal (intention-to-treat) effect of *being offered* the programme, not just an association.

LRPITT11 is the intention-to-treat (ITT) analysis for nonword reading
(`nonword`). The estimand is the total effect of randomised group assignment $G$
on the outcome, using the RCT phase only (time 1 $\rightarrow$ time 2). Nonword
reading is the repo's "post-only" measure and is heavily floored at t2 (~72% of
post-scores at zero), so it takes the pre-specified **floor-rule branch** (#119).

::: {.callout-important title="Floored, post-only outcome — the binary off-floor estimand is PRIMARY"}

An outcome with $\geq 40\%$ of its post-scores at zero at t2 (computed **arm-blind**
and fixed **before** fitting) takes the floor rule. This model:

- uses an **age-only** predictor (no own baseline); the four children with a
  missing t1 nonword baseline are kept, since the age-only model never uses that
  (degenerate) baseline — it is the baseline, not the outcome, that is missing;
- reports the **binary "off-floor at t2"** effect, $\Pr(\text{post} > 0)$, as the
  **PRIMARY** estimand; and
- retains the graded Beta-Binomial $\tau$ only as a flagged, **detection-limited
  SECONDARY**, read alongside the per-arm mover counts and never on its own.

:::

::: {.callout-note title="Identification and sign convention"}

Under the locked causal DAG, $G$ is a randomised root, so the ITT effect $\tau$ is
identified by the **empty adjustment set**; linear age is a precision term only.
**Sign convention:** $G = 1$ is the intervention arm, so a **positive** $\tau$ means
the intervention raises the outcome — here, raises the probability of coming off
the floor.

:::

## Model

PRIMARY (binary off-floor), with $\eta_i = \alpha + \tau\,G_i + \gamma_A\,A_{\text{std},i}$:

$$
[\,N_{\text{post},i} > 0\,] \sim \text{Bernoulli}\big(\sigma(\eta_i)\big).
$$

SECONDARY (graded, detection-limited): the *same* age-only $\eta_i$ with a
Beta-Binomial likelihood on the count, $N_{\text{post},i} \sim \text{BetaBinomial}(N, \mu_i\kappa, (1-\mu_i)\kappa)$, $N = 6$.

![Model diagram (primary)](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_results_floored.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
